events.title: The Round City Rises
events.scene: The caliph al-Mansur has chosen the Tigris for his perfect round capital. Surveyors pace the mud while the family weighs how to plant itself in a city not yet built — early roots run deepest, but the ground is still shifting.
events.researchNote: Baghdad was founded in 762 CE as the Abbasid 'Round City' (Madinat al-Salam) by caliph al-Mansur.

choices.text: Buy land at the city's heart before the walls close
choices.outcome: A gamble on mud that will, within a generation, be the center of the world.

choices.text: Set up as a merchant at the river gates
choices.outcome: Goods flow where the caliph builds. The family's caravans find their first market.

choices.text: Offer the family's service to al-Mansur's administration
choices.outcome: Proximity to the throne — the highest reward and the shortest rope.

events.title: The House of Wisdom Opens Its Doors
events.scene: Translators and astronomers are gathering under the caliph's patronage. The family is offered a seat among them — a chance to bind the name to learning rather than the sword.
events.researchNote: The Bayt al-Hikma (House of Wisdom) grew as a translation and scholarship hub in 8th–9th c. Baghdad.

choices.text: Bind the family to the House of Wisdom
choices.outcome: The name becomes a byword for learning — slow power that outlives swords.

choices.text: Fund the translators as a patron, not a scholar
choices.outcome: Let others hold the pen; the family holds the purse — and the credit.

choices.text: Seek the caliph's court instead — proximity to the throne
choices.outcome: Closer to the throne is closer to the executioner. The family chooses the dangerous game.

events.title: The Caravan to Samarkand
events.scene: A merchant proposes a joint venture: silver and glass east to Samarkand and Chang'an, silk and paper back. The profits are immense and so is the road's appetite for those who underestimate it.
events.researchNote: Abbasid Baghdad sat at the western hub of Silk Road trade reaching Central Asia and Tang China.

choices.text: Fund the long caravan east
choices.outcome: Two years on the road; a fortune in paper and silk if the caravan returns.

choices.text: Keep to the safer river and gulf routes
choices.outcome: Smaller margins, surer returns. The family's coffers fill steadily.

choices.text: Decline — keep capital for land and office
choices.outcome: The family stays close to the city and the court, leaving the road to braver men.

events.title: A Treatise That Travels
events.scene: A work produced under the family's name — on algebra, astronomy, or medicine — is circulating from Córdoba to the eastern frontier. Renown is a kind of power, and a kind of target.
events.researchNote: Abbasid-era scholarship in mathematics, astronomy, and medicine spread across the Islamic world and beyond.

choices.text: Claim intellectual authority over a whole field
choices.outcome: The family's name becomes a school of thought — and a rival to every other.

choices.text: Build a circle of students who carry the name forward
choices.outcome: A quieter immortality: the name survives in a hundred grateful pupils.

choices.text: Sell the knowledge to the highest courts
choices.outcome: Wisdom becomes a commodity. The purists frown; the treasury smiles.

events.title: A Marriage of Houses
events.scene: A Persian vizier's family proposes a match. Their administrative genius and your standing could fuse into something the court cannot ignore — or make the family a target for every rival faction.
events.researchNote: The Persian Barmakid viziers wielded vast administrative power under the early Abbasids before their fall in 803.

choices.text: Accept the marriage and fuse the houses
choices.outcome: Two houses become one engine of state — formidable, and watched.

choices.text: Take a junior partnership, not a marriage
choices.outcome: Aligned but not bound — if the viziers fall, the family may yet survive.

choices.text: Decline — keep the bloodline and the risk yours alone
choices.outcome: The family keeps its own counsel, and its own ceiling.

events.title: The Viziers Fall
events.scene: Overnight the caliph destroys the Barmakid house that ran his empire — arrests, executions, confiscations. Any family tied to them must decide, fast, whether to disavow, defend, or vanish.
events.researchNote: In 803 Harun al-Rashid abruptly destroyed the Barmakid family, executing or imprisoning its leaders.

choices.text: Disavow the viziers and swear fresh loyalty
choices.outcome: The family buys its survival with its honor. The coin spends; the stain lingers.

choices.text: Quietly shelter the viziers' surviving kin
choices.outcome: A dangerous decency. If discovered, the block; if not, a debt of loyalty that may one day pay.

choices.text: Petition for the confiscated offices and estates
choices.outcome: The family rises on its allies' graves. Useful, and never forgiven.

events.title: Two Brothers, One Caliphate
events.scene: The old caliph is dead and his realm is split between two sons. The family must choose which brother to back as the empire slides toward civil war — a wrong guess means the block and the river.
events.researchNote: The Fourth Fitna (809–827) was the civil war between al-Amin and al-Ma'mun after Harun al-Rashid's death.

choices.text: Back the eastern reformer brother and his armies
choices.outcome: A long gamble on the brother historians will call the wiser — if he wins.

choices.text: Stand with the seated caliph in Baghdad
choices.outcome: Loyalty to the man on the throne — until the city falls around him.

choices.text: Hoard grain and gold and wait out the war
choices.outcome: Let the brothers bleed. The family sells bread to both armies.

events.title: The Siege of Baghdad
events.scene: The reformer's armies ring the Round City. Catapults work the walls; the markets starve. The family's choices in the war's opening now come due, for good or ruin.
events.researchNote: The siege of Baghdad (812–813) ended with al-Amin's death and al-Ma'mun's victory in the civil war.

choices.text: Hold with the doomed caliph to the last
choices.outcome: The family becomes a legend of loyalty — sanctified by the loss, a banner for generations.

choices.text: Open a gate for the besiegers and switch sides
choices.outcome: Survival bought with treachery. The new caliph rewards the deed and distrusts the doer.

choices.text: Flee the city with what the family can carry
choices.outcome: The family scatters to the provinces, poorer but alive, waiting for the city to forget.

events.title: The Victor Enters the City
events.scene: Al-Ma'mun rules now, and he remembers who stood where. The family's wartime ledger is read aloud in the new court — and the reading decides the next hundred years.
events.researchNote: Al-Ma'mun (r. 813–833) became caliph after the civil war and presided over a golden age of scholarship.

choices.text: Claim the reward owed for backing the victor
choices.outcome: High office in a golden age — the family's name written into the new order.

choices.text: Seek a quiet pardon for backing the wrong side
choices.outcome: Forgiven, not favored. The family keeps its head and little else.

choices.text: Withdraw into the House of Wisdom, above politics
choices.outcome: Let the court churn. The family's authority moves to where ideas outlast caliphs.

events.title: The Inquisition of Belief
events.scene: The caliph imposes a doctrine and demands every notable swear to it. The family's scholars are summoned to affirm the official creed — or refuse, and be broken for conscience.
events.researchNote: The Mihna (c. 833) was al-Ma'mun's inquisition enforcing the Mu'tazilite doctrine of the created Qur'an.

choices.text: Affirm the caliph's doctrine and keep your posts
choices.outcome: The family bends with the state. Position kept; the traditionalists never forget.

choices.text: Refuse on principle and accept the cost
choices.outcome: A name carved into the conscience of the faithful — at the price of flesh and office.

choices.text: Equivocate — affirm in public, dissent in private
choices.outcome: A scholar's tightrope: survive the inquisition without quite surrendering the soul.

events.title: A Son Strikes Out
events.scene: A younger son, denied the main inheritance, proposes to take a portion of the family's wealth to a provincial capital and found his own branch. It could double the family's reach — or split it into rivals.
events.researchNote: Abbasid-era families often spawned provincial cadet branches as the empire's regions grew semi-autonomous.

choices.text: Endow the cadet branch generously
choices.outcome: The family's banner now flies in two cities. Reach doubled; control halved.

choices.text: Keep the son close and the wealth undivided
choices.outcome: The fortune stays whole; the son stays bitter. A rival made of blood.

choices.text: Send him to the frontier with nothing but a name
choices.outcome: Out of sight, out of the will. Whether he returns a beggar or a warlord is his affair — for now.

events.title: The Measure of a House
events.scene: Al-Ma'mun is dying and the golden age he built will pass to harder men. The family takes stock of what it has become across these seventy years — and what it will carry into the centuries ahead.
events.researchNote: Al-Ma'mun died in 833; the Abbasid golden age continued but the caliphate's central power soon began to fragment.

choices.text: Entrench the family as a permanent power of the realm
choices.outcome: The house becomes a fixture no caliph can rule without — and none fully trusts.

choices.text: Spread the family across the fracturing provinces
choices.outcome: As the center weakens, the family's many roots in the provinces keep it alive.

choices.text: Cement the family's name in scholarship for the ages
choices.outcome: Empires fall; libraries remember. The name will be read long after the caliphate is dust.
